one_line_takeaway: Carefully categorizing and down-sizing safety data (EHD/IHD/MHD) plus token-level masked RL alignment yields "truly safe" LLMs with ~14k safety examples, beating 60k-example DPO on hard safety while avoiding the helpfulness collapse of naive over-alignment.
key_finding: FDC+ADPO with only 14k safety data (10k EHD, 3k IHD, 1k MHD) matches or beats DPO trained on 20k/60k safety data while preserving helpfulness. On the hardest real-world set (Bal-Safe, 10k), FDC+ADPO reached safety 0.9020 on Llama3-8B vs 0.7757 for DPO-60k, and 0.9077 on Qwen2-7B vs 0.7655 for DPO-60k. Crucially, DPO-60k collapsed helpfulness (Llama3 Helpful Avg 0.5979, Qwen2 0.7096) while FDC+ADPO retained it (0.6789 and 0.8044, both slightly above the base models' 0.6728 and 0.7952). On subjective win-tie rate vs base Qwen2-7B on natural data, the adaptive variants won: APPO 0.933, ADPO 0.920, ARS 0.908 vs DPO 0.765, PPO 0.810, RS 0.770, KTO 0.725. EHD safety never exceeded 0.9 by alignment alone (attributed to base-model knowledge limits); adding RAG+self-reflection lifted EHD from 0.7290 to 0.9010 and natural from 0.9020 to 0.9215 while helpfulness stayed ~0.80. The scaling study showed IHD safety saturates after ~3k examples while EHD keeps rising, motivating the fine-grained mixture.
core_contribution: The paper proposes "Equilibrate RLHF," a framework for balancing the helpfulness-safety trade-off during LLM alignment. It has two original components. (1) A Fine-grained Data-centric (FDC) approach that categorizes safety training data into three groups — Explicit Harmful Data / factual-risk (EHD), Implicit Harmful Data / intentional-risk (IHD), and Mixed Risk Data (MHD) — and shows each scales differently, so a small curated mixture (~14k) beats naive large-scale safety data. (2) An Adaptive Message-wise Alignment (AMA) method that applies a gradient/token masking strategy to highlight safety-critical segments instead of treating whole responses as binary chosen/rejected; it is instantiated as three variants (ADPO, APPO, ARS) built on top of DPO/PPO/rejection-sampling. The paper's own motivating finding is that naively scaling safety data pushes models into an "over-safe" (high-refusal) rather than "truly safe" state, degrading helpfulness. Note: DPO, PPO, KTO, rejection sampling, OpenRLHF, dense-RL/token-DPO, and the Schmitt-trigger hysteresis idea are cited prior work the method builds on, not this paper's contributions.
threat_model: The paper defends against two failure modes of aligned LLMs: (1) generating harmful content when prompted (unsafe responses to explicit-harm, implicit-malicious-intent, and mixed prompts), and (2) over-refusal / "over-safe" behavior that declines benign requests and degrades utility. The two identified root causes of unsafe outputs are insufficient safety knowledge in the base model and inadequate alignment. It is an alignment/defense (safety-training) contribution, not an attack paper; the authors explicitly note they do not evaluate against sophisticated red-team/jailbreak attacks (a stated limitation) and flag dual-use risk from releasing harmful example data and generation prompts.
limitations: Stated by the authors: no evaluation against sophisticated red-team/jailbreak attacks (only benchmark evaluation); EHD (factual-risk) safety is bounded by the base model's knowledge and they offer no complete fix — the proposed RAG+self-reflection remedy itself introduces risks from inaccurate external knowledge and data poisoning; the self-developed evaluation dataset cannot be fully released because it contains prohibited harmful entities, limiting reproducibility of that portion. Reviewer-observed: nearly all safety/helpfulness judgments rely on GPT-4 as evaluator (no human safety-scoring baseline reported); the strongest safety gains come on the authors' own non-public Bal-Safe set; the AMA masking formulation depends on an offline reward model / annotator labels and a batch-average baseline that may carry bias; the paper reports only two 7-8B models and no statistical variance. The anonymized artifact link (code/prompts/dataset) is provided.

datasets_used: In-house safety corpus: >1,000k EHD, >300k IHD, >200k MHD
11 helpfulness benchmarks: C-Eval, C3, MMLU, CommonsenseQA, RACE, ARC-C, ARC-E, BBH, HellaSwag, WinoGrande, GSM8K, HumanEval
quantitative_results: Bal-Safe (10k) safety: FDC+ADPO(14k) 0.9020 on Llama3-8B vs DPO-60k 0.7757; 0.9077 on Qwen2-7B vs DPO-60k 0.7655
Helpful Avg: FDC+ADPO Llama3 0.6789 vs base 0.6728 and DPO-60k 0.5979; Qwen2 0.8044 vs base 0.7952 and DPO-60k 0.7096
Win-tie rate vs base Qwen2-7B (natural data): APPO 0.933, ADPO 0.920, ARS 0.908, PPO 0.810, RS 0.770, DPO 0.765, KTO 0.725
RAG+self-reflection lifted EHD 0.7290->0.9010, MHD 0.8835->0.9130, natural 0.9020->0.9215 with helpfulness ~0.80
Optimal safety mixture 14k = 10k EHD + 3k IHD + 1k MHD; IHD safety saturates after ~3k examples
Naive scaling required ~60k safety data for optimal safety but compromised helpfulness
